pillars.name: GovCon Foundations
pillars.purpose: Teach the free, mandatory basics — SAM.gov, UEI, NAICS, size standards, and the 2026 dollar thresholds — so newcomers can legally compete.

pillars.name: Myths & Mistakes
pillars.purpose: Correct the costly misconceptions and self-inflicted errors (paid registration, self-certification, disability-rating myths) that disqualify small businesses.

pillars.name: Opportunity Intelligence
pillars.purpose: Show how to read SAM.gov notice types, filter to biddable work, and mine Award Notices and Sources Sought for competitive intel.

pillars.name: Winning Proposals
pillars.purpose: Break compliance, Section L/M, best-value strategy, past performance, and deadlines into repeatable, screen-proof wins.

pillars.name: SAM.gov Hunter in Action
pillars.purpose: Demonstrate the product matching businesses to live opportunities and scoring fit, in short vertical reels.

ideaBank: Carousel: the 4 federal set-aside certifications, one slide each (WOSB, SDVOSB, HUBZone, 8(a)) — the single hard eligibility test plus the payoff.
Myth reel: WOSB/EDWOSB self-certification died October 15, 2020 — walk the free certify.sba.gov path plus the annual attestation and 3-year exam.
The Rule of Two explainer (FAR 19.502-2) and why the $15K–$350K reserved band is where new contractors should start.
HUBZone: the map is the whole game — principal office in a HUBZone plus 35% of employees residing in one.
SDVOSB myth-buster: even a 0% service-connected rating qualifies, and it is SBA VetCert now, not the VA.
8(a) is a clock, not a badge — 9 years, once in a lifetime, tested on net worth <$850K, 3-yr AGI <$400K, assets <$6.5M.
Timely: 8(a) social-disadvantage rules are in active flux in 2026 — do not apply off old advice; verify at certify.sba.gov.
DUNS is dead, meet your UEI — the free 12-character ID generated inside SAM.gov that never expires (only registration lapses).
Myth: someone quoted you to 'register on SAM.gov' — registration and UEI are 100% free; the real site ends in .gov.
The 5 things to have in hand before you touch SAM.gov (legal name, address, EIN, bank EFT, primary NAICS).
Why your SAM registration got stuck — the exact-match trap: St vs Street, LLC vs L.L.C., IRS name mismatch.
365 days: the annual-renewal reminder graphic — lapse it and you're ineligible for award until reactivated.
UEI vs Registration vs CAGE — stop confusing these three (permanent ID vs annual renewal vs DLA-assigned code).
One NAICS code decides which contracts you even see — and the solicitation's NAICS, not your 'primary,' sets the size standard.
Revenue vs employees: how your NAICS is sized — a 5-year receipts average vs a 24-month headcount average.
Why one great year won't kick you out of small-business status — the 5-year receipts average (Runway Extension Act).
Construction gotcha: 236220 is small up to $45M, but Davis-Bacon (wage-rate) micro-purchases cap at $2,000, not $15,000.
3 reasons your dream contract never showed up in your feed — wrong NAICS, over the size standard, missing certification.
RFP vs RFQ vs IFB — how the award basis (lowest price vs best value) tells you where to spend your bid effort.
'Active' on SAM.gov does not mean biddable — the only three notice types you can actually bid.
The 3 dates that decide if you win: the questions deadline, the amendment date, and the offers-due date and time.
Answering a Sources Sought can flip a full-and-open buy into a small-business set-aside via the Rule of Two.
Award Notices are your competitor cheat sheet — pull the winner, agency, price, and recompete timing from each one.
'Late is late' (FAR 15.208): the rule, the 4:30 p.m. default when no time is stated, and the submit-hours-early habit.
Section L vs Section M — the two pages that decide if you win: organize by L, write to M.
Build a compliance matrix in 5 columns — turn a 100-page RFP into a line-by-line 'shall' checklist (template post).
The 1-page capability statement that gets you in the room — the must-have blocks including UEI, CAGE, NAICS, certs.
No past performance? How not to get screened out — CPARS and the FAR 15.305 'neutral' rule, plus teaming references.
7 ways contractors lose before evaluation even starts — late/wrong portal, page limits, missing form, SAM lapsed.
Product reel: paste your NAICS and watch live, biddable opportunities appear with a fit score on each.
Infographic: how SAM validates your entity now that the Entity Validation Service replaced Dun and Bradstreet on April 4 2022
Carousel: the CAGE code lifecycle, a free 5 character code DLA assigns during SAM registration that stays valid for 5 years
Spotlight: the notarized letter era, why SAM demanded one starting in 2018 and how identity checks replaced it
Tips: finish your annual Representations and Certifications in SAM once and stop re-certifying every bid, per FAR 52.204-8
Reel: your SAM registration cannot send an invoice, you need a separate PIEE and Wide Area Workflow account to get paid
Myth: a foreign company can just register in SAM like a US firm, no, it must get a free NCAGE from NSPA first
Infographic: check the SAM exclusions list before you award a subcontract over 35,000 dollars, per FAR 52.209-6
Reel: the day your Entity Administrator quits, how one person can lock you out of your own SAM record
Spotlight: the EBiz POC, the person in your SAM record who authorizes your organization's users in Grants.gov
Myth: any SAM registration lets you bid contracts, no, picking Financial Assistance Awards Only blocks them
Tips: start your SAM renewal 60 days early because registration dies 365 days after activation and validation lags
Tips: budget time for Login.gov identity verification, it is now required to create or manage a SAM entity
Carousel: when SAM makes you report your top 5 executives pay, the 25 million dollar and 80 percent federal thresholds
Myth: you must do a full SAM registration just to get a UEI, no, request a Unique Entity ID only
Infographic: SAM makes you disclose your immediate and highest level owner, the corporate hierarchy rule in FAR 52.204-17
Reel: marking your SAM record as non public removes it from the public entity search used for market research
Spotlight: one SAM registration covers both federal contracts and grants, you do not register twice
Myth: the NAICS the CO stamped on the solicitation is final. You can appeal it to SBA OHA within 10 calendar days of the solicitation.
Infographic: the size protest clock, you get 5 business days after the apparent-awardee notice to challenge the winner's size.
Reel: you just got acquired, recertify your size within 30 days or you are misrepresenting on every order after.
Infographic: long contracts get a size recheck. Recertify no more than 120 days before the end of year 5, and no more than 120 days before each option is exercised.
Myth: you apply to SBA to become a small business. You self-certify small in SAM, there is no certificate.
Infographic: your size is not just your company, SBA adds every affiliate you control to your receipts and headcount.
Tips: what actually counts as receipts, total income plus cost of goods sold from your tax return, not just top-line revenue.
Infographic: how SBA counts employees, a 24-month average where part-time and temporary staff count the same as full-time.
Carousel: receipts or employees, services and construction are sized on 5-year revenue while most manufacturing is sized on headcount.
Spotlight: the free SBA Size Standards Tool, type your NAICS and see the exact revenue or employee cap in seconds.
Carousel: the dual NAICS reality, you can be small under one code and other than small under another on the same day.
Myth: resellers are locked out of product set-asides. The nonmanufacturer rule lets you bid with 500 or fewer employees.
Reel: growing mid-contract will not kick you out, your size is locked as of the date you submitted your offer.
Tips: the 5-year receipts math done plainly, add your last 5 completed fiscal years of receipts and divide by 5.
Spotlight: the ostensible subcontractor trap, leaning on a big sub for the vital work makes that sub your affiliate.
Spotlight: a mentor-protege joint venture bids as small even when your mentor is a giant.
Infographic: size standards are not frozen, SBA reviews every code at least every 5 years and raised revenue caps for inflation in 2022.
Infographic: EDWOSB adds an economic test that plain WOSB does not, personal net worth under $850K, 3-year average AGI under $400K, and total assets under $6.5M.
Myth: SDVOSB self-certification for set-asides ended January 1, 2023, and you now must hold SBA VetCert to win them.
Spotlight: the highest-officer-position rule, the qualifying owner must own 51% unconditionally and actually run the company day to day, not just hold the stock.
Myth: 51% ownership is enough, a minority partner who keeps veto power over hiring, spending, or quorum creates negative control that can disqualify you.

Carousel: the 5 things that block any SBA certification, one per slide, including a SAM exclusion, debarment, delinquent federal debt, unresolved tax lien, and a non-citizen holding the qualifying 51%.
Spotlight: the 8(a) sole-source superpower, agencies can award an 8(a) firm up to $4.5M in services or $7M in manufacturing with no competition.
Reel: one company can stack 8(a), WOSB, HUBZone, and SDVOSB at the same time, each a separate free application that opens another set-aside lane.
Infographic: HUBZone is 35% at the door and 20% to stay, 35% of employees must live in a HUBZone when you bid, then you attempt to maintain at least 20% during the job.
Myth: a WBENC or state woman-owned certificate does not let you bid federal WOSB set-asides by itself, you must be in the WOSB Federal Contract Program at certify.sba.gov.
Infographic: EDWOSB outranks WOSB on eligibility, an EDWOSB can compete for both EDWOSB and WOSB set-asides while a WOSB can only compete for WOSB set-asides.

Spotlight: the SDVOSB surviving-spouse rule, a spouse can keep the firm's SDVOSB status for up to 10 years if the veteran had a 100% permanent and total rating or died of the service-connected condition.
Tips: the 8(a) two-years-in-business rule and its waiver, you normally need two full years operating but SBA can waive it for proven management, capital, and past performance.
Carousel: every certification runs on its own clock, one slide each, WOSB and SDVOSB 3-year terms, HUBZone annual recertification, and 8(a) a one-time 9-year term.
Tips: what 51% unconditional ownership really means, no options or side agreements that could shift control, and 51% of both the votes and the profit distributions.
Spotlight: the SBA mentor-protege joint venture lets a certified small firm bid set-asides, even 8(a) sole-source, without the big mentor's size sinking the team.
Infographic: two free SBA systems run every cert, certify.sba.gov handles WOSB, 8(a), and HUBZone while VetCert handles SDVOSB and VOSB.
Myth: getting certified does not win you work, a set-aside certification only makes you eligible to compete in the reserved pool, it is not a contract or a bid list.
Infographic: how to read a federal solicitation number (PIID), where the first 6 characters name the issuing office, positions 7 and 8 are the fiscal year, and position 9 flags the instrument type.
Reel: the SF30 amendment you forgot to acknowledge just voided your bid, and here is exactly where you sign it.
Spotlight: the presolicitation notice is your early-warning system, posted before the real solicitation ever drops.
Carousel: Sources Sought vs RFI, both are market research and neither one is a contract you can actually win.
Spotlight: every agency publishes a procurement forecast of upcoming small-business work before anything hits SAM.gov.
Infographic: the Combined Synopsis/Solicitation notice type packs the announcement and the solicitation into one document.
Tips: the synopsis timing rules that tell you how much runway you really get, 15 days before issue and at least 30 days to respond.
Tips: what a Special Notice really signals, from industry days to pre-proposal conferences, and why it is never a solicitation.
Reel: a Justification and Approval notice hands you the name of who is getting a sole-source deal and the reason why.
Infographic: the $25,000 line that decides whether a contract even has to be posted publicly on SAM.gov.
Carousel: the SAM.gov set-aside filter codes decoded, one card each for SBA, 8A, HZC, SDVOSBC, and WOSB.
Tips: PSC is the second filter beginners ignore, and pairing it with your NAICS strips the noise out of your feed.
Tips: add yourself to a notice's Interested Vendors List so primes can find you for teaming on that exact contract.
Reel: the deadline printed on the notice is a trap, always open the latest amendment for the real due date.
Myth: a Cancelled notice means the work is gone for good, when it often returns re-solicited with new terms.
Carousel: the five public stages of a federal buy in order, Forecast, Sources Sought, Presolicitation, Solicitation, and Award.
Myth: FedBizOpps is where you find contracts, when FBO.gov was shut off in 2019 and everything moved to SAM.gov.
Carousel: LPTA vs tradeoff and how FAR 15.101 splits into lowest-price-technically-acceptable (15.101-2) versus the best-value continuum (15.101-1), plus why DoD now avoids LPTA
Carousel: the proposal color-team reviews and what each checks, Pink at half draft, Red for compliance and score, Gold for the executive go, Green for price
Infographic: price reasonableness vs price realism and why one asks if you are too high (FAR 15.404-1(b)) while the other asks if you are too low (FAR 15.404-1(d))
Myth: the reps and certs are just paperwork, and the Section 889 telecom rep (FAR 52.204-24 and 52.204-25) can bar award for using banned Huawei or Hikvision gear anywhere in your company
Infographic: capture vs proposal and why the win is decided in the months before the RFP drops, not in the 30 days after
Myth: amendments are optional reading, and failing to acknowledge one on the SF30 can make your offer unacceptable under FAR 52.215-1
Tips: find the line that says award without discussions, because FAR 52.215-1 lets the government pick a winner off your first proposal with no chance to fix it
Infographic: the 5 CPARS ratings from Exceptional to Unsatisfactory and your right to comment on a bad one for at least 14 days before it locks for 3 years
Tips: no company past performance yet, so cite your key personnel, major subcontractors, and predecessor firm, which FAR 15.305(a)(2)(iii) tells evaluators to consider

Tips: what actually eats your page limit, because evaluators can stop reading at the stated page limit and disregard everything beyond it, and Section L often sets a 12 point minimum font and 1 inch margins
Infographic: Section M must tell you the weighting, because FAR 15.304(e) requires the solicitation to state the relative importance of price versus the non-price factors
Reel: why 'we will comply' loses and how to rewrite it as proof with a name, a number, and a date the evaluator can actually score
Carousel: weakness vs significant weakness vs deficiency, the three FAR 15.001 words that decide whether the evaluator dings you or throws you out
Infographic: your wrap rate is why you lose on price, the multiplier that turns a 30 dollar direct wage into a 60 dollar billed rate through overhead, G and A, and fee
Spotlight: the 2 million dollar line where you must hand over certified cost or pricing data under the Truthful Cost or Pricing Data law (FAR 15.403-4)

Tips: use the questions deadline as a weapon, because a sharp question can reshape a spec, and when the government answers by amendment that response becomes part of the solicitation and binds every offeror equally
Reel: when the pitch is spoken not written, how FAR 15.102 oral presentations can replace or supplement your proposal on a clock with no do-overs
Infographic: GSA Schedule (MAS) decoded, one consolidated Multiple Award Schedule since 2020 with a 5-year base plus three 5-year options that can run 20 years
Myth: a GSA Schedule is a hunting license, not a sales guarantee, and you pay GSA a 0.75% Industrial Funding Fee on every order
Spotlight: 8(a) STARS III, the small-business IT services GWAC that lets agencies direct-order task work from certified 8(a) firms across the government
Infographic: what IDIQ really guarantees is almost nothing, the government commits only to a stated minimum and competes everything above it as task orders
Myth: a BPA is not a contract and orders you nothing, it is a pre-negotiated charge account that skips re-soliciting repeat buys
Myth: signing a teaming agreement does not put you on the contract, FAR 9.601 makes it only a promise to negotiate a future subcontract
Reel: how a small firm bids on a contract ten times its size, an SBA-approved mentor-protege joint venture that SBA will not treat as affiliated
Tips: the rules that keep a mentor-protege JV legal, the protege must perform at least 40% of the work the joint venture does
Infographic: where subcontracts come from, any non-small prime on a contract over $750K ($1.5M for construction) must run an SBA subcontracting plan with small-business goals
Tips: finding a prime to sub under, contact the agency OSDBU and the Small Business Liaison Officer every plan-holding prime is required to name
Carousel: SBIR in 3 phases one slide each, Phase I feasibility, Phase II development, and Phase III commercialization that can be awarded sole-source at any dollar value
Infographic: STTR is not SBIR, STTR forces a partnership where the small business performs at least 40% and a research institution at least 30% of the work
Myth: the All Small Mentor-Protege Program name is gone, SBA merged it with the 8(a) program on November 16, 2020 into one program open to every small business
Spotlight: a mentor can legally own up to 40% of your company, the SBA Mentor-Protege equity rule built to help proteges raise capital
Myth: you cannot just flip the work to a big partner, on a small-business services set-aside you must self-perform at least 50% of the cost
Reel: the ostensible subcontractor trap, if your sub does the primary and vital work SBA counts you as one firm and you lose the set-aside
Myth: iRAPT is some separate system you have to learn, when it is just WAWF's old name and DoD invoicing now lives inside PIEE at piee.eb.mil.
Tips: picking the right WAWF document on a DoD services contract, and why most service vendors file an Invoice 2-in-1 that bundles the invoice and the receiving report.
Infographic: the Prompt Payment Act clock, and how the government owes you interest automatically if it pays a proper invoice more than 30 days late.
Tips: the elements that make an invoice 'proper' so your 30-day payment clock actually starts instead of quietly resetting when it gets kicked back.
Reel: you can win the contract and still not get paid, because the government pulls your bank account straight from your SAM.gov registration.
Myth: the prime can legally sit on your money, when a FAR clause forces accelerated payment to small-business subcontractors.
Infographic: the hidden 7-day acceptance window that runs before your 30-day payment clock even starts.
Tips: never staple an early-payment discount onto a federal invoice unless you truly want it, because the government will take it.
Myth: every federal contract needs a DCAA-approved accounting system, when that requirement only attaches to cost-reimbursement work.
Carousel: the 4 things your books must prove to pass an SF 1408 pre-award accounting survey, one slide each.
Reel: DCAA timekeeping means every employee records every hour every day, including unpaid overtime on salaried staff.
Infographic: the Miller Act, and why federal construction over $150,000 requires both a performance bond and a payment bond.
Spotlight: the SBA Surety Bond Guarantee, the program that gets a new contractor bonded when a surety would otherwise say no.
Myth: Davis-Bacon prevailing wages only hit big jobs, when the trigger is any federal construction contract over $2,000.
Tips: the WH-347 certified payroll on Davis-Bacon jobs is due every week within 7 days of payday, and a false one is a federal crime.
Carousel: direct cost vs indirect cost vs your wrap rate, explained for a first-time cost-type bidder, one slide each.
Infographic: contract closeout, the final invoice and release of claims that actually end a federal job.
Reel: the $10,000 micro-purchase is your easiest first federal sale and a purchase-card micro-buy doesn't even require SAM registration
Infographic: the federal buying ladder from micro-purchase to the Simplified Acquisition Threshold and what each dollar band unlocks for a new vendor
Tips: how to get on a Government Purchase Card holder's radar so your first federal order needs no bid and no competition at all
Reel: use USAspending.gov to see exactly which agencies bought your NAICS last year and for how much before you chase a single notice
Tips: every agency publishes next year's buys in a public procurement forecast and most first-timers never read theirs
Spotlight: every federal agency has a free small-business advocate called the OSDBU and emailing yours is the fastest human way in
Infographic: complete your Dynamic Small Business Search profile so contracting officers find YOU when they look for a set-aside
Tips: pick one agency and go deep instead of chasing every notice, because past performance compounds with the same buyer
Myth: a GSA Schedule is a shortcut to federal sales, when it is really just a hunting license you still have to work
Reel: subcontracting is the back door to your first federal revenue and large primes are legally required to plan to use small businesses
Tips: 3 capability statement mistakes that get you ignored, starting with writing a brochure instead of a targeted one-pager
Infographic: the federal fiscal year ends September 30 and the July to September surge is when a new vendor actually gets a shot
Carousel: a realistic first-contract timeline phase by phase, so you plan for 12 to 18 months not 12 to 18 days
Myth: you can skip acknowledging a solicitation amendment, when failing to do so can make your bid nonresponsive
Reel: your annual reps and certs must be current in SAM or you are ineligible at award even with the lowest price
Myth: rounding your headcount or revenue down to fit a set-aside is harmless, when size misrepresentation is a federal felony
Tips: bid/no-bid discipline that actually wins, only pursue solicitations where you meet 100% of the mandatory Section M requirements

The difference between [A] and [B] is the difference between winning and getting screened out.
New to federal contracting? Start in the $15K–$350K lane. Here is how.
This is free intel hiding in plain sight on SAM.gov —

standingPosts.caption:
New to federal contracting? I put the first steps most people skip into one free starter guide.

Inside:
1) Register in SAM.gov the right way, without paying a reseller a cent.
2) Pick the NAICS codes that actually surface contracts you can win.
3) Find the set-asides you may already qualify for.
4) Read a solicitation and hit every deadline that matters.

It's free. Comment GUIDE below and I'll send it straight to you.

Want to skip ahead and see live opportunities matched to your business? Start free at samgov-hunter.com

adCreatives.primaryText: Stop scrolling SAM.gov for hours. SAM.gov Hunter matches your business to live federal contracts you can actually bid on, scores each one for fit, and helps you write the proposal. Built for small businesses new to government contracting. Start free, no card required.
adCreatives.headline: Find federal contracts that fit your business
adCreatives.description: Matched to your NAICS and certifications. Start free.

launchBatch.headline: The federal small-business lane, 2026
launchBatch.subtitle: The two dollar thresholds that decide how every federal buy is run.

rows.name: Micro-purchase
rows.desc: At/below: direct award, no competitive quotes needed

rows.name: Reserved lane
rows.desc: Generally set aside for small business (Rule of Two)

rows.name: Prime goal
rows.desc: Gov targets 23% of prime dollars to small business

launchBatch.caption:
Two numbers frame every federal purchase, and both went up on October 1, 2025.

Micro-purchase threshold: $15,000. At or below it, an agency can direct-award with minimal competition, often on a government purchase card. It is the fastest on-ramp for a brand-new vendor.

Simplified Acquisition Threshold: $350,000. Below it, buys use streamlined procedures under FAR Part 13.

Here is the part that matters most. The band between the micro-purchase threshold and the SAT is generally reserved for small business under the Rule of Two (FAR 19.502-2). If the contracting officer reasonably expects offers from two or more capable small firms at fair prices, the buy is set aside for small business.

One exception to memorize: for wage-rate (Davis-Bacon) construction, the micro-purchase threshold is still $2,000, not $15,000.

If you are new, this $15K to $350K lane is where you start.

Want to see live opportunities in that lane matched to your business? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

New to this? Comment GUIDE below and I'll send you our free federal contracting starter guide.

Save this so the 2026 numbers are one tap away, and send it to an owner who thinks federal work is out of reach.

launchBatch.headline: You never pay to register in SAM.gov
launchBatch.subtitle: Registration and your UEI are 100% free. Anyone charging a fee is a reseller.
points: Myth: you must pay a service to 'get registered' in SAM.gov.
Fact: entity registration and your 12-character UEI cost $0 through the government.
The UEI replaced the DUNS number on April 4, 2022, and it is generated inside SAM.gov.
The real system ends in .gov; fee sites are third-party resellers, not the government.
Your UEI never expires, but your registration does, every 365 days.
Let it lapse and you are invisible and ineligible for award until it is active again.
launchBatch.caption:
If a website is charging you to 'register in SAM.gov,' close the tab.

Entity registration is free. Your Unique Entity ID (UEI) is free. The government has never charged for either.

A few facts to keep you safe:
- The UEI is a 12-character ID generated inside SAM.gov. It replaced the DUNS number on April 4, 2022. You do not request it from anyone else.
- The real system is sam.gov, and it ends in .gov. Fee sites are third-party resellers, not the government.
- Your UEI never expires, but your registration does, every 365 days. Let it lapse and you are invisible and ineligible for award until it is active again.

Paid services cannot beat the validation clock, either. The delay is the IRS TIN match and CAGE assignment, not a fee.

Set a reminder 60 days before your registration expires and renew it yourself.

Want your active profile matched to live opportunities the moment you are registered? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

New to this? Comment GUIDE below and I'll send you our free federal contracting starter guide.

Save this and share it with anyone about to pay for a free service.

launchBatch.headline: The FAR clause that reserves work for you
launchBatch.subtitle: FAR 19.502-2 is the reason small-business set-asides exist.
points: Hook: one FAR clause decides if a contract is reserved for small firms.
It is called the Rule of Two, FAR 19.502-2.
If the CO reasonably expects offers from 2+ capable small businesses at fair prices...
...the buy generally must be set aside for small business only.
Between $15K and $350K, that reservation is the default.
Answering a Sources Sought makes you evidence that 'the two' exist.
launchBatch.caption:
One FAR clause is the reason small-business set-asides exist. Learn it and you will read every opportunity differently.

It is the Rule of Two, FAR 19.502-2.

The plain-English version: if a contracting officer reasonably expects offers from at least two responsible small businesses at fair market prices, the acquisition generally must be set aside for small business only. Large primes are legally excluded from that competition.

Between the $15,000 micro-purchase threshold and the $350,000 Simplified Acquisition Threshold, that reservation is the default.

This is also why answering a Sources Sought notice matters so much. Your capability statement becomes direct evidence that 'the two' exist, which can shape a buy into a set-aside you are eligible for. Stay silent and you can be designed out before the solicitation ever posts.

Want to see which live opportunities are set aside for your certifications? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

New to this? Comment GUIDE below and I'll send you our free federal contracting starter guide.

Save this and send it to a teammate who thinks they are competing against the whole world.

launchBatch.headline: 5 things to have before you touch SAM.gov
launchBatch.subtitle: The exact-match items that make or break your registration.

rows.name: Legal name
rows.desc: Must match your state formation record exactly

rows.name: Physical address
rows.desc: Consistent everywhere; St vs Street stalls validation

rows.name: TIN + name
rows.desc: Must match IRS files; L.L.C. vs LLC can fail

rows.name: EFT info
rows.desc: Routing + account for contracts; gov pays by EFT

launchBatch.caption:
Most SAM.gov registrations stall for one reason: a field that does not exactly match another record.

Have these five in hand before you start, standardized everywhere:

1) Legal name, matching your state formation record. 'LLC' vs 'L.L.C.' can trigger a rejection.
2) Physical address, identical across records. 'St' vs 'Street' or a missing suite number stalls entity validation.
3) EIN plus legal name, which must match IRS files. The TIN check adds days, and a mismatch can add weeks.
4) Bank EFT info, routing and account numbers. The government pays by electronic funds transfer, so contracts require the full 'All Awards' path.
5) Primary NAICS, so pick codes for work you actually do. Your primary NAICS drives your small-business size standard.

One process note: SAM validates your legal business name, physical address, and incorporation details against outside records, and that entity validation step stalls most people. Requirements have changed over time, so confirm the current steps on FSD.gov before you start.

Plan for 2 to 4 weeks, not minutes. Start before a solicitation is due.

Want live opportunities matched to your NAICS the day you are active? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

launchBatch.headline: You can still self-certify as a WOSB
launchBatch.subtitle: Self-certification for WOSB/EDWOSB set-asides ended October 15, 2020.
points: Myth: check the woman-owned box in SAM and you are a certified WOSB.
Fact: self-certification for WOSB/EDWOSB set-asides ended October 15, 2020.
Certify free at certify.sba.gov or through an SBA-approved third-party certifier.
WOSB = 51% owned AND controlled by one or more women.
EDWOSB adds an economic-disadvantage test and gates different NAICS codes.
A WBENC or state 'woman-owned' cert does NOT win a federal WOSB set-aside.
launchBatch.caption:
'I checked the woman-owned box in SAM, so I am a certified WOSB.' Not for federal set-asides.

Self-certification for the WOSB and EDWOSB Federal Contract Program ended on October 15, 2020. To win a WOSB or EDWOSB set-aside, you must be formally certified.

What that actually looks like:
- Certify for free at certify.sba.gov, or through an SBA-approved third-party certifier.
- WOSB means 51% owned AND controlled by one or more women.
- EDWOSB adds an economic-disadvantage test, and SBA reserves different NAICS codes for EDWOSB vs WOSB, so confirm which applies to your code before you bid.
- You attest annually that you still qualify and undergo a full program examination every three years.

And a common mix-up: a WBENC or state 'woman-owned' certification helps with corporate supplier-diversity buyers, but it does not win a federal WOSB set-aside. That takes SBA certification.

Certification is free. Nobody at SBA charges you to apply.

Want to see which live set-asides your certification qualifies you for? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

launchBatch.headline: 'Active' on SAM.gov doesn't mean biddable
launchBatch.subtitle: Most active notices aren't solicitations you can actually bid.
points: Hook: you filtered to 'Active' and drowned in dead ends.
'Active' also returns Award Notices, Special Notices, and Sources Sought.
Only three notice types are truly biddable:
Solicitation. Combined Synopsis/Solicitation. Presolicitation.
Filter to those three and your feed becomes real work.
Bonus: Award Notices are free intel. Winner, agency, price, recompete timing.
launchBatch.caption:
You filtered SAM.gov to 'Active,' got thousands of results, and gave up. The filter is lying to you, a little.

'Active' also returns notices you cannot bid: Award Notices (someone already won), Special Notices, Justifications, and Sources Sought or RFIs (market research, not solicitations).

The truly biddable notice types are only three:
- Solicitation
- Combined Synopsis/Solicitation
- Presolicitation (opening soon)

Filter to those and your feed becomes real, winnable work.

Then flip the script on the notices you cannot bid. An Award Notice is free competitive intel. It names the winner, the agency, and often the price and scope. Use it to find the recompete when the incumbent's contract ends (many run a base plus options up to about five years) and to target that winner as a teaming partner.

Chasing the wrong notice types is the quiet time-killer for new contractors.

Want a feed that only shows biddable opportunities matched to you? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

launchBatch.headline: Section L vs M: win before you write
launchBatch.subtitle: L tells you HOW to respond; M tells you how you're SCORED.

rows.name: Instructions
rows.desc: Format, page limits, forms, portal, deadline

rows.name: Evaluation
rows.desc: The exact factors your bid is scored against

rows.name: Compliance
rows.desc: Track every 'shall' to where you answer it

rows.name: Tradeoff/LPTA
rows.desc: Pay for quality vs lowest technically acceptable

rows.name: Mirror RFP
rows.desc: Name your sections after Section M factors

rows.name: Hard wall
rows.desc: Late is late (FAR 15.208); submit hours early

launchBatch.caption:
Most losing proposals were beaten before anyone scored them. Compliance beats creativity, every time.

Two sections run the whole game:
- Section L is the instructions. How to respond: format, page limits, required forms, the right portal, the deadline.
- Section M is the evaluation factors. Exactly how you will be scored.

The winning move is simple to say and rare to do: organize by L, write to M. Build your outline from Section L, then make sure every Section M factor is explicitly and visibly addressed, using the RFP's own headings. A time-pressed evaluator should never have to hunt.

Two more essentials:
- Build a compliance matrix. Pull every 'shall' and 'must' into a spreadsheet mapping the requirement to where you answer it. It turns a 100-page RFP into a checklist.
- Know your lane. Best value is a continuum. Tradeoff pays more for higher quality. LPTA awards the lowest price that is merely technically acceptable. Read Section M before you price.

And remember, late is late (FAR 15.208). Submit hours early.

Want the requirements surfaced for you so nothing gets missed? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

launchBatch.headline: SDVOSB myth: you need a high VA rating
launchBatch.subtitle: A 0% service-connected rating still qualifies, and it's SBA VetCert now.
points: Myth: you need a high VA disability rating to be an SDVOSB.
Fact: the disability must be service-connected, and even a 0% rating qualifies.
Certification moved to SBA VetCert in January 2023; the VA no longer does it.
Requires 51% direct ownership AND day-to-day control by service-disabled veteran(s).
SDVOSB self-certification for set-asides was eliminated as of December 22, 2024.
The government-wide SDVOSB prime goal rose from 3% to 5%.
launchBatch.caption:
'My VA disability rating is not high enough to be an SDVOSB.' That is not how it works.

For the Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business program, the disability must be service-connected, but there is no minimum rating. Even a 0% service-connected rating qualifies.

What actually matters:
- 51% direct ownership AND day-to-day control by one or more service-disabled veterans.
- Certification moved to SBA's VetCert program in January 2023, and the VA no longer certifies firms. Apply free at veterans.certify.sba.gov.
- Self-certification for SDVOSB set-asides was eliminated as of December 22, 2024. Checking a box in SAM is not enough.

The demand is real. The government-wide SDVOSB prime goal rose from 3% to 5%, and self-certified awards no longer count toward it, which pushes agencies toward certified firms like yours.

Certification is free. Do not pay anyone to file the application itself.

Want to see the SDVOSB set-asides open right now? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

launchBatch.headline: Award Notices are your competitor cheat sheet
launchBatch.subtitle: You can't bid them, but they're the best free intel on SAM.gov.
points: An Award Notice names the winner, the agency, and often the price and scope.
Find the recompete. Most contracts run a base plus options up to about 5 years.
Target the winner as a teaming or subcontracting partner.
Benchmark realistic pricing against what actually won.
Treat every award in your NAICS as free market research.
launchBatch.caption:
You cannot bid an Award Notice, someone already won. But it might be the most valuable thing in your SAM.gov feed.

Every Award Notice names the winner, the agency, and often the price and scope. Read it like an intelligence brief:

1) Find the recompete. Most contracts run a base period plus options, often up to about five years. Note when the incumbent's term ends and start positioning now, not when the next solicitation drops.
2) Target the winner as a partner. A prime that just won work in your lane is a teaming or subcontracting lead, a proven way for newer firms to build the relevant past performance evaluators reward.
3) Benchmark pricing. What actually won tells you what 'realistic' looks like far better than a guess.
4) Map the buyer. Repeated awards in your NAICS show which agencies actually spend in your space.

Treat every award in your NAICS as free market research.

Want live opportunities and their award history matched to your business? Start free at samgov-hunter.com.

reelScripts.title: Your NAICS code runs your whole feed
reelScripts.hook: One 6-digit code decides which federal contracts you'll ever see.
scenes: Text on screen: 'Every federal solicitation is tagged with a NAICS code.'
The contracting officer assigns it based on the principal purpose of the work (FAR 19.102).
Reveal: the solicitation's NAICS, not your 'primary' code, sets the size standard that governs that bid.
Split screen: small under one NAICS, 'other than small' under another, the same day.
Tip: list the codes for work you actually do. Code-stuffing hurts you.
End card: match live opportunities to your codes. Start free at samgov-hunter.com
reelScripts.caption: Here is the most misunderstood thing in federal contracting. Your NAICS code is the filter that decides which opportunities reach you and whether you even qualify. Each solicitation lists a NAICS code, and SBA's size standard for THAT code decides if you count as small for THAT bid, so you can be small under one code and not another on the same day. Set the wrong codes and matching contracts never surface. Want your codes matched to live opportunities with a fit score on each? Start free at samgov-hunter.com. New to this? Comment GUIDE and I'll send you our free starter guide. Save this and send it to someone still guessing at their codes. #govcon #NAICS #smallbusiness #SAMgov #governmentcontracting #setaside

reelScripts.title: 8(a) is a clock, not a badge
reelScripts.hook: The 8(a) program is powerful, and most people misunderstand what it actually is.
scenes: Text: '8(a) = a 9-year business-development program. Once in a lifetime.'
Show the payoff: sole-source access plus set-aside competitions.
Eligibility gate: personal net worth under $850K.
Caution card: the social-disadvantage rules are changing in 2026, so verify at certify.sba.gov before you apply.
End card: Start free at samgov-hunter.com
reelScripts.caption: 8(a) can transform a small business, and it runs on a strict clock. Nine years, once in a lifetime, for firms owned 51% by individuals who are socially AND economically disadvantaged, with real economic tests: net worth under $850K, 3-year average AGI under $400K, total assets under $6.5M. And the social-disadvantage rules are in active flux in 2026, so never apply off old advice. Check certify.sba.gov for current guidance. Want to see the 8(a) set-asides open right now? Start free at samgov-hunter.com. New to this? Comment GUIDE and I'll send you our free starter guide. reelScripts.title: The 3 dates that decide if you win
reelScripts.hook: You can write a brilliant proposal and still lose on a calendar.
scenes: Text: 'Every solicitation hides three deadlines. Miss the wrong one and you're out.'
Date 1: the questions (Q&A) deadline, earlier than you think, and the one people miss.
Date 2: the amendment date. An SF30 can move the due date or change the work, so acknowledge every amendment or your bid can be tossed.
Date 3: the offers-due date and time. Late is late (FAR 15.208). No time stated? It defaults to 4:30 p.m. local.
Rule of thumb: submit hours early, never at the deadline.
End card: never miss a date. Start free at samgov-hunter.com
reelScripts.caption: Compliance beats creativity, and timing is compliance. Three dates run every bid: the questions deadline (earlier than the proposal deadline, and the one most people miss), the amendment date (acknowledge every SF30 or your bid can be found nonresponsive), and the offers-due date and time (late is late under FAR 15.208, and a missing time defaults to 4:30 p.m. local). Build a buffer and submit hours early. Want deadlines surfaced on every opportunity you're tracking? Start free at samgov-hunter.com. New to this?
